From өл- (öl-) + -үм (-üm). Cognates with Kazakh өлім (ölım), Turkish ölüm, Uzbek o‘lim.
From earlier *yaztuq, from Proto-Turkic *yaŕtuk (“pillow”). Cognate with Turkish yastık (“pillow”).
From Kipchak طشاق (tašaq), Kazakh тасақ (tasaq), etc.
From Proto-Turkic *bal. Cognate with Azerbaijani bal, Bashkir бал (bal), Kazakh бал (bal), Tatar бал (bal), Turkish bal, Turkmen bal, Uzbek bol, etc.

From Proto-Turkic *kāpuk (“bark; skin; shell”). Cognate with Kazakh қабық (qabyq),
Southern Altai кабык (kabïk), Bashkir ҡабыҡ (qabıq), Tatar кабык (qabıq), Crimean Tatar qabuq, Kumyk къабукъ (qabuq),
Uzbek qobiq (“skin, shell”),
Turkish kabuk (“shell”),
Tuvan хавык (xavık, “husk”),
Chuvash хупӑ (hup̬ă, “bark”), etc.
From Proto-Turkic *kur (“belt”). Compare Southern Altai кур (kur).
From илүү (ilüü) + -гич (-gic). Cognate to Southern Altai илгиш (ilgiš), etc.

From Proto-Common Turkic *kadï-.
Cognate with
Old Turkic [script needed] (qaðïš, “belt”);
Kazakh қайыс (qaiys, “rawhide”);
Bashkir ҡайыш (qayış, “belt”),
Southern Altai кайыш (kayïš, “belt”)
Uzbek qayish (“belt”),
Khakas хаас (xaas, “belt”),
Yakut хатыс (qatïs, “leather strap/belt”),
etc.
From Proto-Turkic. Influenced by Old East Slavic.

senses: diminutive of топ (top) (female garment for the upper body)

etymology: Compare to Southern Altai ойык (oyïk, “hollow”), etc.

etymology: From Proto-Turkic *kudruk (“tail”). Cognate with Old Turkic 𐰴𐰆𐰑𐰺𐰆𐰸 (qudruq, “tail”), Turkish kuyruk (“tail”).
A Mongolic borrowing: compare Mongolian хүрэн (xüren, “brown”) and see Persian کرند (korand) for more.

From Proto-Turkic *yaman (“bad”).
Cognate with
Old Uyghur [script needed] (jaman, “bad”);
Bashkir яман (yaman), Karakalpak жаман (jaman) / jaman, Kazakh жаман (jaman),
Karachay-Balkar аман (aman),
Uzbek yomon,
Azerbaijani, Turkish yaman, etc.

Cognate with
Old Uyghur [script needed] (quδurčuq, “doll”);
Bashkir ҡурсаҡ (qursaq),
Kazakh қуыршақ (quyrşaq),
Tatar курчак (qurçaq),
Southern Altai корчок (korčok, “humpback”),
Kumyk къурчакъ (qurçaq),
Uzbek qoʻgʻirchoq,
Uyghur қорчақ (qorchaq)/قورچاق (qorchaq),
Turkmen gurjak (“doll”), etc.

dividend (а number that is to be divided by another)
dart (used in the game of darts)
etymology: Borrowed from Classical Persian نیزه (nayza, “spear, lance, javelin”).

From Old Turkic [script needed] (*aqča, “money, coin”). Cognate with
Tatar акча (aqça),
Bashkir аҡса (aqsa),
Kazakh ақша (aqşa),
Southern Altai акча (akča),
Kumyk акъча (aqça),
Karachay-Balkar ахча (axça),
Crimean Tatar aqça,
Khakas ахча (axça),
Shor ақча,
Tuvan акша (akşa, “money”),
Chuvash укҫа (ukśa, “coin”), etc.

From Proto-Turkic *teriŋ (“deep”).
Cognate with Azerbaijani dərin (“deep”), Bashkir тәрән (tərən, “deep”), Kazakh терең (tereñ, “deep”), Turkish derin (“deep”), etc.

etymology: From Proto-Turkic *čï̄pïn, compare Azerbaijani çibin, Bashkir себен (seben), Crimean Tatar çibin.

Borrowed from Arabic خَبَر (ḵabar). Cognate with Karachay-Balkar хапар (xapar), Azerbaijani xəbər, Bashkir хәбәр (xəbər), Kazakh хабар (xabar), Khakas хабар (xabar), Kumyk хабар (xabar), Nogai хабар (xabar), Ottoman Turkish خبر (haber), Shor қабар, Tatar хәбәр (xäbär), Turkmen habar, Uyghur خەۋەر (xewer), Uzbek xabar, Chuvash хыпар (hyp̬ar), etc.
From Persian قیمت, from Arabic قِيمَة (qīma).

senses: Dalmatia (a historical region of Croatia, on the eastern coast of the Adriatic Sea)

etymology:
From *tāmāq ~ *tāmğāq "throat".
Old Uyghur [script needed] (tamğaq, “throat, pharynx”);
Southern Altai тамак (tamak, “throat, food”),
Kazakh тамақ (tamaq, “throat, food”),
Kumyk тамакъ (tamaq, “throat”),
Uzbek tomoq (“throat, food”),
Uyghur تاماق (tamaq, “food”),
Turkmen damak (“throat; food”),
Khakas тамах (tamax, “throat; food”),
Yakut тамах (tamaq, “throat”).
From бас- (bas-) + -кыч (-kıc). Compare to Southern Altai баскыш (baskïš), etc.
Deverbal from бөлүү (bölüü, “to decompose, discriminate”). Compare to Southern Altai бӧлиш (böliš, “division”), Kumyk бёлмек (bölmek), etc.
From Proto-Turkic *kur (“belt”). Compare Southern Altai кур (kur).
From илүү (ilüü) + -гич (-gic). Cognate to Southern Altai илгиш (ilgiš), etc.

From Proto-Common Turkic *kadï-.
Cognate with
Old Turkic [script needed] (qaðïš, “belt”);
Kazakh қайыс (qaiys, “rawhide”);
Bashkir ҡайыш (qayış, “belt”),
Southern Altai кайыш (kayïš, “belt”)
Uzbek qayish (“belt”),
Khakas хаас (xaas, “belt”),

Borrowed from Persian جلب, from Arabic جَلَب (jalab, “merchendise”). Cognates with Kazakh жәлеп (jälep).
From Proto-Turkic *kāĺ. Cognate to Southern Altai каш (kaš, “shore”).
